channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Blue Micromoon Rises in Sunday’s Early Skies
item.description: The second full moon of May will look smaller and dimmer than usual.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: A micromoon rising over North Macedonia last year. A blue moon is when a second full moon occurs in a single calendar month, while a micromoon is when it is at its farthest distance from Earth.

item.title: A Hidden Treasure of Rare Snake Specimens
item.description: In the foothills of the Ecuadorean Amazon, a 101-year-old farmer and a young scientist turned an amateur collection into a scientific survey of one of the most diverse snake habitats on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:description: Manuel Genaro Peñafiel Flores with some of his collection of snakes at home in Mera, Ecuador.

item.title: What’s Next for Blue Origin After Rocket Explosion
item.description: Jeff Bezos was gaining ground on Elon Musk’s SpaceX and Starlink. Thursday’s rocket explosion on a launchpad creates a major setback.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:description: Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket exploded on its launchpad during a test.

item.title: Happy, Bronx Zoo Elephant at Center of Animal Rights Case, Is Dead at 55
item.description: Activists had sued in a bid to secure her the fundamental human right to bodily liberty. Zoo officials said she was well cared for and called the lawsuit frivolous.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan
item.media:description: Happy the elephant had lived at the Bronx Zoo since 1977.

item.title: Can New Technology Help Save North Atlantic Right Whales?
item.description: Innovative systems to keep ships from hitting North Atlantic right whales are coming into use. The Trump administration is weighing whether they can replace a bedrock protection.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Fish Sleep a Lot Like Us. (They Even Nap.)
item.description: By tracking eye movements in zebrafish, researchers identified four different types of sleep, analogous to the complex sleep patterns of humans and other animals.
item.dc:creator: Sara Novak

item.title: Why the Ebola and Hantavirus Outbreaks Have Confounded Scientists
item.description: The types of Ebola and hantavirus worrying officials are very different from the species identified decades ago, raising new questions about how to respond.
item.dc:creator: Carl Zimmer

item.title: A Fiery Visitor From Space Photo-Bombs an Erupting Volcano
item.description: For a split second, a fireball outshone the rivers of molten lava flowing from the Philippines’ most active volcano.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: NASA’s Moon Base Plan Adds Two Rovers for Its Astronauts
item.description: The contracts, announced on Tuesday, are part of the space agency’s efforts to establish a moon base.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: 2025 Wildfires Were the Costliest Ever, Researchers Say
item.description: Severe, hard-to-control blazes in densely populated areas like Los Angeles drove the year’s record losses.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pacific Palisades, Calif., on Jan. 7, 2025. Fires in the Los Angeles area last year burned least 90 square miles, killing at least 31 people and displacing more than 150,000.

item.title: By September, Nearly a Third of Americans Will Live in States With Legal Aid in Dying
item.description: Despite widespread support in polls, the number of people who actually go through with the practice remains very small.
item.dc:creator: Paula Span

item.title: China’s Rise in Drug Development Looms Over U.S.
item.description: Clinical trials in China are getting attention at an international oncology gathering in Chicago. China’s surging biotechnology industry is fueling alarm that U.S. dominance in the field is waning.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:description: The American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meeting in Chicago last year. One of the conference’s coveted headliners this year is a presentation of a clinical trial conducted only in China.

item.title: A Clean Energy PAC Helped Beat Chip Roy, and Now It Has New Targets
item.description: A PAC representing wind and solar energy interests spent $1.1 million to boost the Republican primary opponent of Chip Roy, an opponent of renewables. Now they are trying to save a Republican ally in Iowa.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of renewable energy investors paid for this projection on the side of the Energy Department building in Washington gloating over Representative Chip Roy’s defeat in a Republican primary for Texas attorney general.

item.title: What Exactly Caused That Loud Boom in South Carolina?
item.description: A huge noise rattled part of the state on Thursday. Multiple theories have been put forward, but the mystery remains.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: Blue Origin Rocket Blows Up on Florida Launchpad During Test
item.description: The rocket, built by the Jeff Bezos-owned space company, was to carry 48 satellites into space. Blue Origin reported on social media that “all personnel have been accounted for.”
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Malcolm Denemark/USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters. Video by Nailah Morgan
item.media:description: The Blue Origin launchpad seen in flames from Cocoa Beach, Fla. on Thursday night.

item.title: Suit Says Black Infants Were Subjected to Experimental Vaccine Without Consent
item.description: The babies were part of a vaccine trial for a respiratory virus in the 1960s and died shortly after. Their families said they had been unaware of the trial until recently.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:description: Relatives of Ross Otto Hambrick and Victor Marcellus King, infants were given a vaccine for respiratory syncytial virus, or R.S.V., in the 1960s, with the lawyer Ben Crump and members of his legal team.

item.title: Greenpeace’s Long War With a Pipeline Titan Enters a Strange New Phase
item.description: This month, a North Dakota court barred Greenpeace from saying what it wanted in a European court, an unusual move. The environmental group says it is forging ahead.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: A court building in Amsterdam. The North Dakota Supreme Court this month barred Greenpeace International from making claims against Energy Transfer in a Dutch countersuit.

item.title: Genetic Scores are Booming. But Will Anti-Discrimination Laws Cover Your DNA?
item.description: As predictive medicine advances, legal scholars warn that decades-old federal guidelines could set up a potential clash between your genes and your job.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:description: A scanning electron micrograph of human chromosomes. Polygenic risk scoring, increasingly popular in personal medicine, uses an individual’s genome to estimate that person’s likelihood of developing a specific disease.

item.title: New York City’s Manhattanhenge is Back: When, Where and How to Watch
item.description: On Friday, some streets in Manhattan will get a lot more crowded as people gather for a second time to watch the city’s unique solar spectacle.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller
item.media:description: Partly cloudy skies are in the forecast for Friday evening, which could obstruct the view of the sunset, according to the National Weather Service.

item.title: U.S. Aims to Give Cold War Plutonium to Start-Ups for Nuclear Fuel
item.description: Companies say it’s a better way to dispose of the Cold War-era material — and fix a shortage of nuclear fuel. But the plan has also faced criticism from nonproliferation experts.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Work with radioactive materials at a plutonium facility at the at Los Alamos National Laboratory in 1978.

item.title: Eli Lilly to Buy 3 Vaccine Developers
item.description: The drugmaker said it would spend up to $4 billion to acquire small companies exploring vaccines for shingles, Epstein-Barr virus and other pathogens.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: Eli Lilly has a long history in infectious diseases.

item.title: Scientists Ditched a Scary Climate Scenario. What Now?
item.description: While global warming is still a threat, the decision to back away from a worst-case outlook raises questions about whether some risks have been overstated.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Eric Niiler
item.media:description: A wildfire burning in the Simi Valley of California this month.

item.title: A Powerful H.I.V. Drug Lands in Zambia. But Will It Reach Those Who Need It?
item.description: Lenacapavir, which protects people from H.I.V. infection with twice-yearly shots, is arriving in a country where the health system has been hollowed out by American aid cuts.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: Health workers promoted a new H.I.V. prevention method at the University of Zambia in Lusaka in March and encouraged students to sign up to get it.

item.title: Where to See the Total Solar Eclipse on Aug. 12
item.description: On Aug. 12, parts of Greenland, Iceland, Spain and Portugal will experience the thrill of daytime darkness. Here’s where you can witness the cosmic spectacle.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Dowling
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Total darkness will last a little over two minutes in parts of the Westfjords region of Iceland.

item.title: Threat of Explosion From Toxic Chemical Tank Lessened, Officials Say
item.description: The risk of a large explosion has been averted, officials say. But a smaller blast remains possible, and 16,000 people remain displaced.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault, Heather Knight, Shawn Hubler and Christina Morales
item.media:description: An aerial view of water being sprayed onto a tank at GKN Aerospace in Garden Grove, Calif., on Saturday.

item.title: One-and-Done Heart Disease Prevention? Scientists Show It May Be Possible.
item.description: A single infusion of an experimental gene-editing drug seemed to reduce LDL long-term in a small trial. The results may point to something “curative,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:description: A colored transmission electron micrograph of low-density lipoprotein, or LDL, particles.
